question: St Paul's teaching on spiritual gifts was directed to _______
solution:
Paul's teaching on spiritual gifts is primarily found in 1 Corinthians 12–14, where he explains the different gifts given by the Holy Spirit and their role in the church. He emphasizes that these gifts should be used for the common good and that love (charity) is the greatest of all virtues (1 Corinthians 13).

Biblical Reference (1 Corinthians 12:4-7, RSV):

"Now there are varieties of gifts, but the same Spirit; and there are varieties of service, but the same Lord; and there are varieties of working, but it is the same God who inspires them all in every one. To each is given the manifestation of the Spirit for the common good."

Paul discusses gifts like prophecy, healing, speaking in tongues, and interpretation of tongues, encouraging unity in diversity within the church.

question: When Paul came to Rome, the first set of people to Whom he preached were the
solution:
When Paul arrived in Rome, he first preached to the Jews living there. This was in line with his usual practice of preaching to the Jews first before turning to the Gentiles.

Biblical Reference (Acts 28:17, RSV):

"After three days he called together the local leaders of the Jews; and when they had gathered, he said to them, ‘Brethren, though I had done nothing against the people or the customs of our fathers, yet I was delivered as a prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans.’”

Paul explained his situation to them and later preached about Jesus Christ, but some of the Jews rejected his message. As a result, Paul then turned to the Gentiles (Acts 28:28).

question: Which of the following kings said, 'Behold your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt?
solution:
The statement “Behold your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt” was made by King Jeroboam after he made two golden calves and set them up in Bethel and Dan. He did this to stop the Israelites from going to Jerusalem (in the southern kingdom of Judah) to worship, thereby consolidating his rule over the northern kingdom of Israel.

1 Kings 12: 28 (RSV):

“So the king took counsel, and made two calves of gold. And he said to the people, ‘You have gone up to Jerusalem long enough. Behold your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt.’”
— 1 Kings 12:28 (RSV)

question: Which of these kings did not assassinate his predecessor but was assassinated by his successor?
solution: Pekahiah was the son of Menahem and became king of Israel after his father's death. He reigned for two years in Samaria. During his reign, he did not kill his predecessor but continued the policies of his father. However, Pekahiah was assassinated by his own commander-in-chief, Pekah son of Remaliah, who then became the king of Israel. This sequence of events indicates that Pekahiah did not assassinate his predecessor but was himself assassinated by his successor.

8 Now at Lystra there was a man sitting, who could not use his feet; he was a cripple from birth, who had never walked. 9 He listened to Paul speaking; and Paul, looking intently at him and seeing that he had faith to be made well, 10 said in a loud voice, “Stand upright on your feet.” And he sprang up and walked. 11 And when the crowds saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voices, saying in Lycao′nian, “The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!” 12 Barnabas they called Zeus, and Paul, because he was the chief speaker, they called Hermes.

question: Pilate delivered Jesus to be crucified because he
solution: Pilate's decision to deliver Jesus to be crucified was largely influenced by the pressure from the crowd. The chief priests and elders, who stirred up the crowd, demanded Jesus' crucifixion, and Pilate, wanting to maintain order and avoid a potential riot, yielded to their demands. Pilate himself found no fault in Jesus and even washed his hands publicly to symbolize his innocence in the matter, but he ultimately chose to prioritize political expediency and placating the crowd over justice.

question: Believers today are partakers of the New Covenant through
solution:
Believers today are partakers of the New Covenant through the gift of the Holy Spirit. According to Acts 2:38 and Romans 8:9-11, the Holy Spirit is given to all believers as a sign of the New Covenant. The Holy Spirit indwells believers, guiding and empowering them to live according to God's will and serving as a seal of their redemption.

The New Covenant is not about following rituals or rules in the same way the Old Covenant required, but about receiving the Holy Spirit, who enables believers to live in relationship with God.

question: The secon temptation according to the gospel of Luke was
solution:
The temptations of Christ are identical in the two accounts, but Matthew records them as:

3. Worship Satan to receive great power.

Luke changes the order of the temptations slightly to:

1. Turn stones into bread.
2. Worship Satan.
3. Leap from the temple pinnacle.

options.a: worship Satan to receive great power.

question: _______ deceitfully took 150 pounds of silver in two bags and two changes of clothes against the wish of his master.
solution: (2 Kings 5:1) Elisha the prophet healed Naaman of his leprosy by the power of the Lord (verse 14), and Naaman praised God and offered Elisha a gift, which Elisha refused to accept (verse 16). Naaman departed for Syria, but Elisha's servant Gehazi ran after him and told a lie in order to get Naaman's gift for himself. In his lie, Gehazi invoked his master's name, making it appear as if Elisha wanted the gift after all: "My master sent me to say, 'Two young men from the company of the prophets have just come to me from the hill country of Ephraim. Please give them a talent of silver and two sets of clothing'" (verse 22). Naaman was only too glad to comply—he was happy to be able to give something out of gratitude for his healing—and he urged Gehazi to take twice as much silver as he had asked for.

question: The gospel of ______ began with the baptism of Jesus
solution:
The Gospel of Mark begins with the baptism of Jesus. 

The Proclamation of John the Baptist

1 The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God.[a]

2 As it is written in Isaiah the prophet,[b]

“Behold, I send my messenger before thy face,
who shall prepare thy way;
3 the voice of one crying in the wilderness:
Prepare the way of the Lord,
make his paths straight—”

4 John the baptizer appeared[c] in the wilderness, preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. 5 And there went out to him all the country of Judea, and all the people of Jerusalem; and they were baptized by him in the river Jordan, confessing their sins. 6 Now John was clothed with camel’s hair, and had a leather girdle around his waist, and ate locusts and wild honey. 7 And he preached, saying, “After me comes he who is mightier than I, the thong of whose sandals I am not worthy to stoop down and untie. 8 I have baptized you with water; but he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit.”

The Baptism of Jesus

9 In those days Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan. 10 And when he came up out of the water, immediately he saw the heavens opened and the Spirit descending upon him like a dove; 11 and a voice came from heaven, “Thou art my beloved Son;[d] with thee I am well pleased.”
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

solution:

Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in newness of life.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Paul encouraged Philemon to receive Onesimus as ______.
solution: Paul wrote to Philemon to encourage him to receive Onesimus back as a brother in the gospel without the severe punishments that would usually be inflicted on runaway slaves. Paul even offered to make up any financial loss Onesimus had caused Philemon to suffer.

question: Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall ___________.
solution: Matthew 5:8

solution: 1 Corinthians 12:28

question: According to Paul's letter to the Philippians, Christ demonstrated His humility by
solution:
In Philippians 2:6-8 (RSV), Paul explains how Christ demonstrated His humility:

"Who, though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men. And being found in human form he humbled himself and became obedient unto death, even death on a cross.

This passage emphasises that Christ, though being God, humbled Himself by becoming human and even submitting to the humiliating death on the cross.

options.a: washing the feet of his disciples
options.c: coming to earth as a servant

question: Naboth refused to give up his vineyard to King Ahab because
solution: 1 Kings 21:1-16 states that Naboth owned a vineyard, in proximity to King Ahab's palace in the city of Jezreel. Because of this, Ahab desired to acquire the vineyard so that he could use it for a vegetable (or herb) garden. Since he inherited the land from his ancestors, Naboth refused to sell it to Ahab.

options.d: it was his only source of income

question: "Do not be afraid, Zechariah, for your prayer has been heard. Your wife Elizabeth will bear you a son" The son here refers to
solution: Luke 1:13

question: In Paul's Epistle to the Galatians, as long as an heir is a child, he is under
solution:
Galatians 4.
What I am saying is that as long as the heir is a child, he is no different from a slave, although he owns the whole estate. He is subject to guardians and trustees until the time set by his father.

question: After his resurrection,Jesus first appeared to
solution:
Mark 16:9-11
Jesus appears to Mary Magdalene, who is now described as someone whom Jesus healed from possession by seven demons. She then "tells the other disciples" what she saw, but no one believes her.

solution: James 1:6-8

solution: Acts 2:1–31

question: Who was responsible for the death of Uriah?
solution: In the morning David wrote a letter to Joab and sent it by the hand of Uriah. In the letter he wrote, "Set Uriah in the forefront of the hardest fighting, and then draw back from him, that he may be struck down, and die.(2 Samuel 11:14-15)
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: ''He who hears you hears me, and he who rejects you rejects me .....'' This was addressed to

options.a: the 12 whom He sent out to preach

question: Jesus asked his disciples not to disclose their vision of the transfiguration until his
solution: As Jesus came down from the Mount of Transfiguration with Peter, James, and John, He told His three disciples not to disclose the wonderful vision they had seen there, until after He had risen from the dead.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question:
"Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!"
When was this statement made?

question: Who accompanied Paul on his First Missionary journey?
solution: The first missionary journey began in about 45 A.D. From Antioch, Barnabas and Saul traveled about sixteen miles to the coast, to the port at Seleucia Pieria.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The Lord told Solomon his kingdom would be divided after his death because
solution:
The Lord told Solomon that his kingdom would be divided after his death because he followed other gods and allowed idolatry in Israel, despite his earlier devotion. This was in response to Solomon's disobedience after God had blessed him with wisdom and wealth.

Biblical Reference (1 Kings 11:9-13, RSV):

"And the Lord was angry with Solomon because his heart had turned away from the Lord, the God of Israel, who had appeared to him twice, and had commanded him concerning this matter, that he should not go after other gods; but he did not keep what the Lord commanded. Therefore the Lord said to Solomon, ‘Since this has been your mind, and you have not kept my covenant and my statutes which I have commanded you, I will surely tear the kingdom from you and will give it to your servant. Yet for the sake of David your father I will not do it in your days, but I will tear it out of the hand of your son.’”

Solomon's foreign wives led him to worship foreign gods, causing a breach in his covenant with God.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The Babylonian Exile was a consequence of
solution: The Babylonian Exile was a consequence of the Israelites' disobedience to God and their social injustices. The Bible records that the Israelites had turned away from God and were worshipping idols, and their society was marked by injustice and oppression. God allowed the Babylonians to conquer them as a punishment for their sins.

options.a: The division of the kingdom into Israel and Judah
options.b: The Israelites' disobedience to God and their social injustices
options.c: The Israelites' refusal to pay tribute to Babylon
options.d: The Assyrian invasion and destruction of Jerusalem

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which prophet confronted King Ahab and challenged the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel?
solution: The prophet Elijah confronted King Ahab and challenged the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel. This is recorded in the Book of Kings in the Bible. Elijah's challenge was a demonstration of God's power over the false gods of Baal.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which prophet warned the people of Nineveh about God's impending judgment?
solution: The prophet Jonah was the one who warned the people of Nineveh about God's impending judgment. This is recorded in the Book of Jonah in the Bible.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The downfall of the Northern Kingdom of Israel was mainly due to
solution: The downfall of the Northern Kingdom of Israel was mainly due to the rejection of the Mosaic Law. This led to moral decay and spiritual decline, which eventually led to their downfall.

options.c: The rise of powerful neighboring kingdoms

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The Book of Lamentations is traditionally attributed to which prophet and describes the sorrow and mourning over the fall of Jerusalem?
solution: The Book of Lamentations is traditionally attributed to the prophet Jeremiah. It describes the sorrow and mourning over the fall of Jerusalem in 586 B.C. by the Babylonians. Jeremiah, often referred to as the 'weeping prophet', laments over the destruction of Jerusalem and the Temple, expressing the pain and confusion of the people.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: According to the Apostle Peter, what will happen to the heavens and the earth on the day of the Lord's return?
solution: According to the Apostle Peter, the heavens and the earth will be destroyed by fire on the day of the Lord's return. This is mentioned in 2 Peter 3:10, where Peter describes the day of the Lord as a time when the heavens will disappear with a roar, the elements will be destroyed by fire, and the earth and everything in it will be laid bare.

options.a: They will merge into a new dimension
options.b: They will be destroyed by fire
options.c: They will be transformed into paradise

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which prophet confronted the people with the challenge of rebuilding the temple after the exile?
solution: The prophet Haggai confronted the people with the challenge of rebuilding the temple after the exile. In the book of Haggai, the prophet encourages the people to rebuild the temple, promising that God's glory will fill the new temple and bring prosperity.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In which book of the New Testament do we find the account of the Apostle Paul's conversion and subsequent missionary journeys?
solution: The account of the Apostle Paul's conversion and subsequent missionary journeys is found in the 'Acts of the Apostles'. The book of Acts, written by Luke, provides a detailed account of Paul's dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus and his subsequent missionary journeys.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The story of Gideon showcases leadership qualities like humility and reliance on divine guidance during
solution: The story of Gideon is found in the book of Judges in the Bible. Gideon was a judge who led the Israelites to victory over the Midianites. His story is characterized by his humility and reliance on divine guidance, which were key to his leadership during the period of Judges. The other options refer to different periods or events in the Bible.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which Gospel gives the most detailed account of Jesus' temptation?
solution: The Gospel of Matthew gives the most detailed account of Jesus' temptation. In Matthew 4:1-11, the writer provides a detailed narrative of how Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In the book of Acts, which event symbolizes the inclusion of the Gentiles into the new covenant?
solution: In the book of Acts, the baptism of Cornelius, a Gentile, symbolizes the inclusion of the Gentiles into the new covenant (Acts 10). The other options refer to other significant events in the book of Acts, but they do not symbolize the inclusion of the Gentiles into the new covenant.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In which epistle does the apostle Paul extensively discuss the concept of "New Life in Christ"?
solution: The apostle Paul extensively discusses the concept of 'New Life in Christ' in the Epistle to the Ephesians. This concept refers to the transformation that occurs in a person's life when they accept Jesus Christ as their Lord and Savior. It does not feature prominently in the Revelation, Acts of the Apostles, or Corinthians.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which city served as a central hub for the Early Church's fellowship and growth?
solution: Jerusalem served as a central hub for the Early Church's fellowship and growth. After the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ, the apostles stayed in Jerusalem where they preached and converted many to Christianity.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: According to the Gospel accounts, what was the first miracle performed by Jesus?
solution: According to the Gospel of John (John 2:1-11), the first miracle performed by Jesus was turning water into wine at a wedding in Cana. The other options refer to other miracles performed by Jesus, but they were not his first.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which Gospel emphasizes the role of the Holy Spirit as the Comforter or Advocate?
solution: The Gospel of John emphasizes the role of the Holy Spirit as the Comforter or Advocate. In John 14:16, Jesus promises to ask the Father to give another Advocate to help and be with the disciples forever.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which prophet warned the Israelites about the consequences of their disobedience?
solution: Isaiah is one of the major prophets in the Bible who warned the Israelites about the consequences of their disobedience to God. He preached extensively about their sins and urged them to repent, or else face judgment, which included exile and destruction. Isaiah’s prophecies include both warnings and promises, with significant emphasis on the coming of a Messiah and the restoration of Israel.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: How did the early Christian community in Acts make decisions about the distribution of resources to the needy?
solution: In the book of Acts, the early Christian community appointed deacons to oversee the distribution of resources to the needy. This was done to ensure fairness and to allow the apostles to focus on prayer and the ministry of the word (Acts 6:1-4).

options.a: They left it to chance and luck
options.b: They appointed deacons to oversee the process
options.c: They gave everything to the apostles for distribution
options.d: They relied on the guidance of local rulers

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Jesus often referred to Himself as the "Good Shepherd." Which Gospel contains this metaphor?
solution: The metaphor of Jesus referring to Himself as the 'Good Shepherd' is found in the Gospel of John, specifically in John 10:11 and 10:14. In these verses, Jesus uses the metaphor to illustrate His care and sacrifice for His followers.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which prophet is associated with the "valley of dry bones" vision, symbolizing the restoration of Israel from exile?
solution: The prophet associated with the "valley of dry bones" vision is Ezekiel. In the book of Ezekiel, chapter 37, Ezekiel has a vision where he is set in the middle of a valley full of dry bones. He prophesies to these bones that they will hear the word of the Lord, and the Lord will cause breath to enter the bones and they will come to life, symbolizing the restoration of Israel from exile.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: What decision did Pilate's wife make when she had a dream about Jesus?
solution: In Matthew 27:19, Pilate's wife sent him a message warning him not to have anything to do with Jesus because she had suffered a great deal in a dream because of him. This shows that she warned Pilate not to get involved with Jesus.

options.a: She kept the dream to herself
options.b: She warned Pilate not to get involved
options.c: She sought advice from the chief priests
options.d: She urged Pilate to release Jesus immediately

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: During the trial before Pontius Pilate, which Roman custom allowed the release of one prisoner during Passover?
solution: It was a period of Peace.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In a diverse and multicultural society, civic responsibility includes
solution: In a diverse and multicultural society, civic responsibility includes embracing diversity and promoting inclusivity. This means respecting and valuing the differences among people, including their backgrounds, cultures, and beliefs, and promoting an inclusive society where everyone feels valued and accepted. It does not involve isolating oneself from people of different backgrounds, promoting stereotypes and prejudice, or disrespecting other cultures and beliefs.

options.b: Isolating oneself from people of different backgrounds

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The prophet who emphasized the concept of "the day of the Lord" as a time of judgment and salvation was
solution: The prophet Joel emphasized the concept of "the day of the Lord" as a time of judgment and salvation. In the book of Joel, the 'day of the Lord' is described as a time when God will judge His enemies and save His people.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In the Gospel of Matthew, which group of religious leaders were particularly hostile towards Jesus?
solution: The Pharisees were particularly hostile towards Jesus, according to the Gospel of Matthew. They often challenged Jesus' authority and teachings, leading to many confrontations.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The prophet who spoke extensively about the restoration of Israel and the coming of the Messiah was
solution: The prophet Malachi spoke extensively about the restoration of Israel and the coming of the Messiah. In the book of Malachi, he prophesies about the coming of the 'messenger' who will prepare the way for the Lord. This is interpreted as a prophecy about the coming of John the Baptist and Jesus Christ.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which prophet had a vision of a "wheel within a wheel," symbolizing God's divine presence and sovereignty?
solution: The prophet Ezekiel had a vision of a "wheel within a wheel," which is recorded in the Book of Ezekiel in the Bible. This vision symbolizes God's divine presence and sovereignty, showing that God is omnipresent and has control over all things.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In which Gospel do we find the Parable of the Good Samaritan?
solution: The Parable of the Good Samaritan is found in the Gospel of Luke, specifically in Luke 10:25-37. In this parable, Jesus uses a Samaritan, considered an outsider by the Jews, as an example of a neighbor who showed mercy to a man who was robbed and beaten.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The new covenant is based on
solution: The new covenant, as described in the New Testament of the Bible, is based on faith in Jesus Christ and His sacrifice. This is a shift from the old covenant, which was based on adherence to the Law (the Ten Commandments and other laws). The other options refer to aspects of the old covenant or to human efforts, which are not the basis of the new covenant.

options.c: Faith in Jesus Christ and His sacrifice

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: What significant event led to the division of languages among humankind?
solution: The significant event that led to the division of languages among humankind was the Tower of Babel. This is recorded in Genesis 11:1-9 in the Bible.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Nahum's prophecy mainly focused on the impending doom of
solution: Nahum's prophecy mainly focused on the impending doom of Nineveh. The Book of Nahum in the Bible is essentially an oracle of judgment against the city of Nineveh, which was the capital of the Assyrian Empire.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The Apostle Paul's life in Acts exemplifies God's sovereignty through
solution: The Apostle Paul's life in Acts exemplifies God's sovereignty through his transformation from a persecutor to a proclaimer of the Gospel. Before his conversion, Paul (then known as Saul) was a zealous persecutor of Christians. However, after his encounter with Jesus on the road to Damascus, he became one of the most fervent proclaimers of the Gospel (Acts 9:1-22).

options.a: His unwavering devotion to Jewish traditions
options.b: His escape from imprisonment through supernatural means
options.c: His transformation from a persecutor to a proclaimer of the Gospel
options.d: His ability to convert everyone he encountered

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Setting boundaries and rules for children helps them
solution: Setting boundaries and rules for children helps them develop a sense of discipline and responsibility. Boundaries provide a safe space for children to explore and understand their environment, while rules guide their behavior and actions, teaching them what is acceptable and what is not. This helps inculcate a sense of discipline and responsibility in them.

options.a: Develop a sense of discipline and responsibility
options.d: Develop a fear of their parents

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: According to the Gospels, what was the profession of Peter and Andrew before becoming disciples of Jesus?
solution: Peter and Andrew were fishermen before they became disciples of Jesus. This is stated in the Gospels, where Jesus tells them to follow him and he will make them 'fishers of men'.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which event marked the beginning of the Early Church's fellowship?
solution: The event that marked the beginning of the Early Church's fellowship was Pentecost. This is when the Holy Spirit descended upon the apostles, as recorded in Acts 2. The resurrection of Jesus, the crucifixion of Jesus, and the Last Supper were all significant events, but they did not mark the beginning of the church's fellowship.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: How do the Gospels portray God's sovereignty concerning human salvation?
solution: The Gospels portray God's sovereignty concerning human salvation as being based on God's grace and mercy. This is evident in passages such as Ephesians 2:8-9, which states that it is by grace through faith that we are saved, not by works, so that no one can boast. This shows that salvation is a gift from God, not something that can be earned through human effort or achievements.

options.a: It is only accessible to a select few
options.b: It is exclusively determined by human choices
options.c: It is based on God's grace and mercy
options.d: It depends on human effort and achievements

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Who were the three disciples who witnessed the Transfiguration with Jesus?
solution: The three disciples who witnessed the Transfiguration with Jesus were Peter, James, and John. This event is recorded in all three Synoptic Gospels (Matthew 17:1-9, Mark 9:2-8, Luke 9:28-36).
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Leadership qualities such as integrity and honesty are illustrated in the life of
solution: Leadership qualities such as integrity and honesty are illustrated in the life of Joseph. This is evident in his life story as recorded in the Book of Genesis in the Bible.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The law was given to humanity primarily to
solution: The law was given to humanity primarily to guide them in righteous living. The law provides a moral and ethical framework for humans to follow, which helps them distinguish between right and wrong and guides their actions and decisions. It does not save them from sin or establish a covenant with God, but it does bring condemnation when it is broken.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In the Gospel of Matthew, Jesus walks on water to meet His disciples in a boat. Which disciple attempts to walk on water but begins to sink?
solution: In the Gospel of Matthew (Matthew 14:22-33), Peter is the disciple who attempts to walk on water to meet Jesus but begins to sink because of his doubt. The other options refer to other disciples who did not attempt to walk on water in this account.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: What event in the Old Testament marked the beginning of the Exodus of the Israelites from Egypt, eventually leading to the Ten Commandments?
solution: The event in the Old Testament that marked the beginning of the Exodus of the Israelites from Egypt, eventually leading to the Ten Commandments, was the Passover. During the Passover, God spared the Israelites from the final plague in Egypt - the death of the firstborn - and this led to Pharaoh releasing the Israelites from slavery, marking the beginning of the Exodus.

options.d: Building of the Ark of the Covenant

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which Gospel includes the account of Jesus washing the disciples' feet during the Last Supper?
solution: The account of Jesus washing the disciples' feet during the Last Supper is found in the Gospel of John, specifically John 13:1-17. This act was a demonstration of humility and service, setting an example for his disciples to follow.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Divine love in Christianity is primarily expressed through
solution: Divine love in Christianity is primarily expressed through God's grace and mercy. This is evident in the Christian belief that God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to die for the sins of humanity, offering forgiveness and eternal life to all who believe in Him. This act of sacrifice is seen as the ultimate expression of God's love, grace, and mercy.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which prophet from the Old Testament strongly condemned the greed of the wealthy and powerful?
solution: The prophet Amos strongly condemned the greed of the wealthy and powerful. Amos was a shepherd and a farmer before God called him to be a prophet. He was an outsider from Judah who was called to deliver a message to Israel. His message was one of justice and concern for the poor and oppressed.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: What was the name of the high priest who played a prominent role in opposing the early Christian movement?
solution: Caiaphas was the high priest who played a prominent role in opposing the early Christian movement. He was the one who plotted to kill Jesus after the raising of Lazarus, as mentioned in John 11:49-50. He also presided over the Sanhedrin trial of Jesus.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: According to the Bible, how many days after his resurrection did Jesus ascend into heaven?
solution: According to the Bible, Jesus ascended into heaven 40 days after his resurrection. This is recorded in Acts 1:3 where it is stated that Jesus presented himself alive to his disciples by many proofs, appearing to them during forty days and speaking about the kingdom of God.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Who was the first Gentile to receive the Holy Spirit and be baptized, as mentioned in Acts?
solution: The first Gentile to receive the Holy Spirit and be baptized, as mentioned in the book of Acts, was Cornelius. This event is recorded in Acts 10, where Peter is shown in a vision that the Gospel is for all people, not just the Jews, leading to the baptism of Cornelius and his household.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In which Gospel does Jesus say, "Before Abraham was, I am"?
solution: The statement, "Before Abraham was, I am" is found in the Gospel of John, specifically John 8:58. Here, Jesus is declaring His eternal existence and His identity as God. The phrase 'I am' is a direct reference to God's name in Exodus 3:14, where God revealed Himself to Moses as 'I AM WHO I AM'.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which epistle encourages believers to "eagerly desire spiritual gifts, especially the gift of prophecy"?
solution: The quote 'eagerly desire spiritual gifts, especially the gift of prophecy' is from 1 Corinthians 14:1. Therefore, the epistle that encourages believers to eagerly desire spiritual gifts, especially the gift of prophecy, is 1 Corinthians.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which virtue is often mentioned alongside impartiality in the selected epistles?
solution: Humility is often mentioned alongside impartiality in the selected epistles. Humility is a virtue that involves recognizing one's own limitations and placing the needs of others before one's own. It is closely related to impartiality, as a humble person does not show favoritism or partiality.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In the context of prayer, what does "intercession" mean?
solution: Intercession in the context of prayer refers to the act of intervening on behalf of another through prayer. This is often done by praying for others, making requests to God on their behalf. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Praying for others'.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: David's skill in playing the harp made him a favorite musician in the court of which king?
solution: David's skill in playing the harp made him a favorite musician in the court of King Saul. This is documented in the Bible, specifically in the book of 1 Samuel 16:23. Whenever King Saul was troubled by an evil spirit, David would play the harp and the king would feel better.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: True religion in Christianity emphasizes the importance of
solution: True religion in Christianity emphasizes the importance of unity among believers. This is based on the teachings of Jesus Christ who prayed for unity among his followers in John 17:21. The other options do not accurately represent the emphasis of true religion in Christianity.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In 1 Corinthians 12, Paul compares the Church to what metaphorical body?
solution: In 1 Corinthians 12, Paul uses the metaphor of a human body to describe the Church. He explains that just as a body, though one, has many parts, but all its many parts form one body, so it is with Christ. Therefore, the correct answer is 'A human body'.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: What is a prominent theme in St. James' Epistles?
solution: The Epistle of James emphasizes practical aspects of Christian conduct. It stresses the ethical and moral responsibilities of Christians in dealing with social issues. The prominent theme in St. James' Epistles is 'Faith and works'. James 2:14-26, in particular, emphasizes that faith without works is dead.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Who was the prophet that warned Israel about the impending Babylonian exile?
solution: The prophet Jeremiah warned Israel about the impending Babylonian exile. Jeremiah prophesied during the last forty years of Judah, the southern kingdom, warning God's people of their coming fate if they did not turn from their sins.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In the Gospel of Mark, which group of people did Jesus specifically mention that we should love with all our hearts?
solution: In the Gospel of Mark, Jesus specifically mentions that we should love our enemies. This is found in Mark 12:30-31, where Jesus states the greatest commandment is to love God with all our heart, soul, mind, and strength, and the second is to love our neighbor as ourselves. In the context of the New Testament, 'neighbor' includes enemies.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In the epistles, corruption is often linked to which of the following vices?
solution: In the epistles, corruption is often linked to vices such as greed and selfishness. These vices are seen as moral corruption and are often warned against in the teachings of the apostles. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Greed and selfishness'.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: David was known for his musical talents and was credited for composing many
solution: David was known for his musical talents and was credited for composing many Psalms. The Book of Psalms in the Bible contains many songs and poems attributed to David. These psalms express a range of human emotions and were often used as hymns in Jewish and Christian worship.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Where did Peter make the "Great Confession"?
solution: Peter made the 'Great Confession' near the Sea of Galilee. This event is recorded in Matthew 16:13-20, Mark 8:27-30, and Luke 9:18-21. Peter confesses that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God, a pivotal moment in the Gospels.

options.c: At the foot of the Cross

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: What significant event led to the division of the kingdom of Israel into the northern and southern kingdoms?
solution: The significant event that led to the division of the kingdom of Israel into the northern and southern kingdoms was the reign of King Solomon. After Solomon's death, his son Rehoboam became king. Rehoboam's harsh rule led to a rebellion, resulting in the division of the kingdom into the northern kingdom of Israel and the southern kingdom of Judah.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In which Gospel(s) do we find an account of the triumphal entry of Jesus into Jerusalem?
solution: The triumphal entry of Jesus into Jerusalem is an event that is described in all four canonical Gospels. In Matthew 21:1–11, Mark 11:1–11, Luke 19:28–44, and John 12:12–19.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: What is the key to effective prayer according to the selected epistles?
solution: According to the selected epistles, the key to effective prayer is faith. This is based on the belief that God hears and answers prayers, and that He is able to do exceedingly abundantly above all that we ask or think, according to the power that works in us. Therefore, when praying, one must have faith that God will answer in His own time and way.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: According to the epistles, what is the root cause of showing partiality to others?
solution: According to the epistles, the root cause of showing partiality to others is favoring those who can offer personal benefits. This is a form of selfishness and is contrary to the teachings of the Bible, which encourages believers to treat everyone equally and without favoritism.

options.b: Fear of being judged by others
options.d: Favoring those who can offer personal benefits

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In which epistle does the apostle Peter remind believers that they are part of a "chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation" while facing persecution?
solution: The apostle Peter reminds believers that they are part of a 'chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation' in the face of persecution in the epistle of 1 Peter, specifically in 1 Peter 2:9. Therefore, the correct answer is '1 Peter'.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: What was the central theme of Ezekiel's prophecies during the exile?
solution: The central theme of Ezekiel's prophecies during the exile was God's judgment and the hope of restoration. Ezekiel was a prophet during the time of the Babylonian exile. He prophesied about the destruction of Jerusalem and the temple, but he also spoke of a future time when God would restore His people and their place of worship.

options.a: God's judgment and the hope of restoration
options.b: The promise of a new messianic king
options.d: The restoration of the temple in Jerusalem

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: How many books are there in the New Testament of the Bible?
solution: There are 27 books in the New Testament of the Bible. These books include the four Gospels, the Acts of the Apostles, the Epistles, and the book of Revelation.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: How many recorded appearances did Jesus make after his resurrection?
solution: Jesus made 7 recorded appearances after his resurrection. These appearances were to Mary Magdalene, the other women, two disciples on the road to Emmaus, Peter, the ten disciples (Thomas was absent), the eleven disciples (Thomas was present), and the seven disciples by the Sea of Tiberias.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Who was the first king of the divided kingdom of Israel?
solution: The first king of the divided kingdom of Israel was Jeroboam. After the death of King Solomon, the kingdom of Israel was divided into two: the northern kingdom of Israel and the southern kingdom of Judah. Jeroboam became the king of the northern kingdom.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The Epistle of Romans is significant in discussing the contrast between law and grace. Who is the author of the Epistle to the Romans?
solution: The Epistle to the Romans, often shortened to Romans, is the sixth book in the New Testament. Biblical scholars agree that it was composed by the Apostle Paul to explain that salvation is offered through the gospel of Jesus Christ. Hence, Paul is the author of the Epistle to the Romans.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Why did the Pharisees criticize Jesus during the triumphal entry?
solution: During the triumphal entry, the Pharisees criticized Jesus because they believed He was claiming to be the Messiah. This is evident in Luke 19:39 where the Pharisees in the crowd said to Jesus, 'Teacher, rebuke your disciples!' They were upset that the crowd was proclaiming Jesus as the Messiah.

options.a: They were jealous of Jesus' popularity
options.b: They thought Jesus was a Roman sympathizer
options.c: They believed Jesus was claiming to be the Messiah
options.d: They were upset about the disruption caused by the crowd

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In Acts, what event caused the dispersion of Christians and led to the spread of the Gospel among Gentiles?
solution: The event that caused the dispersion of Christians and led to the spread of the Gospel among Gentiles was the stoning of Stephen. This event is recorded in Acts 7:54-60 and Acts 8:1. After Stephen's death, a great persecution broke out against the church in Jerusalem, and all except the apostles were scattered throughout Judea and Samaria.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which epistle addresses the issue of moral purity and living a sanctified life within the Christian community?
solution: The First Epistle to Timothy addresses the issue of moral purity and living a sanctified life within the Christian community. In this letter, Paul provides guidance to Timothy, a young leader in the church, on how to conduct himself and lead the church in a manner that is pleasing to God. This includes instructions on moral purity and living a sanctified life.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In which Gospel do we find the story of the "Good Samaritan" who showed love and compassion to a wounded man?
solution: The story of the 'Good Samaritan' is found in the Gospel of Luke, specifically in Luke 10:25-37. In this parable, Jesus uses the example of a Samaritan, who was considered an outsider, to illustrate the concept of 'neighbor' and to demonstrate that love and compassion should extend beyond social or religious boundaries.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which epistle emphasizes the importance of love and unity among believers in the Christian community?
solution: The Epistle to the Ephesians emphasizes the importance of love and unity among believers in the Christian community. It is a letter written by the Apostle Paul to the church in Ephesus, and it focuses on the themes of unity in the body of Christ, the importance of love, and the spiritual growth of believers.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which apostle famously doubted Jesus' resurrection until he saw the wounds in His hands and side?
solution: The apostle who famously doubted Jesus' resurrection until he saw the wounds in His hands and side was Thomas. This event is recorded in John 20:24-29, where Jesus appeared to his disciples after his resurrection, but Thomas was not present. When the others told him about it, he refused to believe until he could see and touch Jesus' wounds. When Jesus appeared again, Thomas was present and believed.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The period of the "Return from Exile" is associated with which Persian King's decree?
solution: The period of the 'Return from Exile' is associated with King Cyrus's decree. According to the Bible, specifically the book of Ezra, King Cyrus of Persia issued a decree that allowed the Jews to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the temple.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which epistle contains the famous analogy of the Church as the "body of Christ" and the members as individual parts?
solution: The analogy of the Church as the 'body of Christ' and the members as individual parts is found in the epistle of 1 Corinthians, specifically in 1 Corinthians 12:12-27. Therefore, the correct answer is '1 Corinthians'.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Which biblical passage is known as the "Lord's Prayer" and contains a model of prayer and guidance?
solution: The biblical passage known as the 'Lord's Prayer' is found in Matthew 6:9-13. This prayer was taught by Jesus to his disciples as a model for how they should pray. It contains petitions for daily needs, forgiveness, and deliverance from temptation.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The message of repentance and forgiveness was a central theme in the prophecies directed to which city?
solution: The message of repentance and forgiveness was a central theme in the prophecies directed to Nineveh. This is evident in the book of Jonah, where Jonah was sent by God to warn the people of Nineveh of their impending destruction unless they repented of their sins.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The southern kingdom of Judah experienced a significant religious reform under the leadership of which righteous king?
solution: The southern kingdom of Judah experienced a significant religious reform under the leadership of King Hezekiah. Hezekiah is known for his efforts to abolish idolatry in his kingdom, as documented in the Bible in the books of 2 Kings 18-20 and 2 Chronicles 29-32.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The people of nineveh declared fasting and mourning at Jonah's message of God's pending destruction and God forgave them. This shows God will forgive nations that?
solution: The king and the people of Nineveh responded positively to Jonah's message. They abandoned their evil behavior and turned to God. When Jonah began to proclaim God's plan to destroy Nineveh within "forty days," the people of Nineveh believed in God.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: "..he who touches the earth and it melts..?"  Who was prophet Amos reffering to in the statement above?
solution:
In the Bible, Amos 9:5 says, "The Lord, the God of Hosts, he who touches the earth and it melts". The prophet Amos is referring to God in this statement. 

Amos 9:5 is a description of God's power and glory. The verse conveys that God is all-powerful and omniscient, and that he has absolute control over the earth and its inhabitants. The verse also emphasizes that God's judgment is inevitable and that there is no escape from his scrutiny.

options.d: The creator of the heavenly hosts

solution: Onesimus, a slave that had departed from his master Philemon, was returning with this epistle wherein Paul asked Philemon to receive him as a "brother beloved" (Philemon 1:9–17).

question: Jesus' statement that he is the Light of the world means that?
solution: Jesus' statement is a metaphor that refers to the light of truth, word, and eternal life. "I am the light of the world" meaning We can't see our future, direction, or destination, but Jesus can. Like the lighthouse guided the ship, Jesus directs us toward God's light. The light of Christ guides us through the darkness of sin, life, and struggle both spiritually and literally.

question: When the babylonians were still attacking, King Zedekiah tried to escape but was caught by the army of the?
solution:
King Zedekiah attempted to escape during the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem, but he was captured by the Chaldeans, who were the ruling dynasty of Babylon at the time.

In Jeremiah 39:5 (RSV):

"But the army of the Chaldeans pursued them, and overtook Zedekiah in the plains of Jericho; and when they had taken him, they brought him up to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon at Riblah, in the land of Hamath; and he passed sentence upon him."

Although the Babylonians were the dominant empire, their ruling class was the Chaldeans, making this the correct answer.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Jesus drove away the people selling and buying in the temple, declaring that it is a house of prayer. This shows?
solution: “When it was almost time for the Jewish Passover, Jesus went up to Jerusalem. In the temple courts he found people selling cattle, sheep and doves, and others sitting at tables exchanging money. So he made a whip out of cords, and drove all from the temple courts, both sheep and cattle; he scattered the coins of the money changers and overturned their tables. To those who sold doves he said, ‘Get these out of here! Stop turning my Father’s house into a market!’ His disciples remembered that it is written: ‘Zeal for your house will consume me’” (John 2:13-17).

options.a: Only Christians can sell in church

options.c: Animals should not be sold in the church

question: The vision of the bones by prophet ezekiel potrays?
solution:
In the Bible, the vision of the bones in Ezekiel 37 portrays God's plan for the restoration of Israel and the power of God to revive his people: 

  • The vision

    In the vision, Ezekiel sees a valley full of dry bones and is commanded to prophesy to them. The bones come together, flesh and skin develop, and breath enters them, causing them to stand up as an army. 

  • The meaning

    The vision symbolizes the Israelites who were in captivity and felt their nation had died. The vision shows that God's power, not the circumstances of the people, would allow Israel to have a new life. The vision also conveys that God can give life to what is dead, and that he can revive his people and make them a mighty army.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: According to paul in his epistle to the Galatians, believers cannot be justified by the works of the law but through?
solution:
According to Paul in his epistle to the Galatians, believers are justified by faith in Jesus Christ, not by the works of the law: 

  • Galatians 2:16
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In the parable of the wheat and tares, the harvest time refers to the?
solution:
In the parable of the wheat and tares, harvest time refers to the end of the age when people will be separated into their eternal destiny. 

The parable of the wheat and tares is found in the Bible in Matthew 13:24-30 and Mark 4:26-29. In the parable, a farmer sows good seeds in his field, but an enemy sows weeds among the wheat. The farmer waits for the wheat and weeds to grow together, and at harvest time, he tells his harvesters to separate the weeds from the wheat. The weeds are tied into bundles and burned, while the wheat is gathered into the barn.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: "And the king stood by the pillar and made a covenant before the Lord.." which king was referred to in this statement?
solution:
The statement "And the king stood by the pillar and made a covenant before the Lord..." refers to King Josiah and is found in 2 Kings 23:3 (RSV).

  • Josiah was a righteous king of Judah who led a major religious reform.
  • After discovering the Book of the Law in the Temple (2 Kings 22:8–13), he realized that the people had strayed from God's commandments.
  • He then renewed the covenant with God by standing by the pillar in the Temple and pledging to follow the Lord's commands (2 Kings 23:3).
  • This event marked one of the greatest religious reforms in Israel’s history, as Josiah removed idolatry and restored true worship.

Since Josiah is the king who made the covenant before the Lord, the correct answer is B. Josiah.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The major achievement of King Solomon during his reign was in his?
solution:
King Solomon's major achievement during his reign was the construction of the Temple of Jerusalem, which was the holy dwelling place of Yahweh: 

  • The Temple of Jerusalem

    Built around 950 BCE, the Temple was the crowning achievement of Solomon's vast building program. It's also known as "Solomon's Temple" or the "First Temple". 

  • Other construction projects

    Solomon also built his own palace, ship fleets, trade routes, and stables. Some historians believe he also built the gates of Megiddo.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: It was paul's belief that spiritual gifts are of great advantage to the church because they?
solution:
Paul believes that spiritual gifts are of great advantage to the Church because they promote growth in diverse ways. Spiritual gifts are special abilities that Christians receive from the Holy Spirit to help them serve God and others. 

 Paul believed that spiritual gifts are beneficial to the church because they:

  • Build the church: Spiritual gifts help build up the church and strengthen and encourage believers. 
  • Equip believers: Spiritual gifts prepare believers to serve in the Kingdom work. 
  • Bring unity: Spiritual gifts bring unity of faith, knowledge of Christ, and maturity in Christ. 
  • Fulfill the church's mission: Spiritual gifts empower the church's mission to evangelize the world.

options.a: Enhance the beauty of the church
options.d: Help the individual to be active

question: "As the Lord the God of Israel lives, before whom i stand, there shall be neither dew nor rain these years except by my word". This statement was made by?
solution: Elijah the Tishbite said, "As the Lord the God of Israel lives, before whom I stand, there shall be neither dew nor rain these years except by my word" in 1 Kings 17:1 of the Bible. Elijah was a prophet from the settlers in Gilead.

question: Peter exhorts the elders to tend the flock of God that is in their charge not by constraint but?

question: Paul's teachings in Ephesians 5 on a fragrant offering describes how
solution: In Ephesians 5:2, Paul describes Christ's sacrifice as a "fragrant offering" to God, which is a metaphor for how believers can imitate Christ's love and live a life that pleases God.

options.b: To give acceptable worship to God
options.c: Christ loved us and died for us

question: The two prophets present with Jesus during the transfiguration were?
solution:
The two prophets who appeared with Jesus during the Transfiguration were Moses and Elijah:

  • Moses: Symbolized the Law
  • Elijah: Symbolized the prophets 

The Transfiguration is described in the Gospels, particularly in Luke 9:28-36. The event is considered the peak of Jesus' public ministry. 

During the Transfiguration,: 

  • Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a mountain to pray 
  • Jesus' face changed, and his clothes became as bright as lightning 
  • Moses and Elijah appeared in glorious splendor 
  • Moses and Elijah spoke about Jesus' departure, which he was about to bring to fulfillment at Jerusalem 
  • The appearance of Moses and Elijah symbolized that Jesus is their successor and has fulfilled both 
  • The appearance of Moses and Elijah demonstrated that Jesus is the fulfillment of “the Law and the Prophets” .
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: A devoted man who feared God with all his household, who liberally gave alms to people and prayed constantly to God was?
solution: Cornelius is a man in the Bible who was described as a devout man who feared God with all his household, gave alms generously to the people, and prayed to God always. Acts 10:2.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Gehazi secretly collected gifts from Naaman that Elisha had rejected. This act could be described as?
solution:
In the Bible, Gehazi's act of secretly collecting gifts from Naaman after Elisha rejected them could be described as greed and deception: 

  • Greed 
    Gehazi was deceived by the spirit of covetousness and dishonored Elisha's ministry. 

  • Deception
    Gehazi lied to Naaman, telling him that Elisha had requested money and clothing for two young men. Naaman was pleased to give Gehazi more than he asked for. Gehazi then hid the gifts in his house and lied to Elisha about where he had been. 

As punishment for his disobedience, Elisha cursed Gehazi with Naaman's leprosy, and Gehazi left Elisha's presence a leper. 

The story of Gehazi and Naaman is a warning against pursuing dishonest gain. The Bible teaches that God sees everything and will judge accordingly.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Moses struck the rock for the people of Israel to drink water by his?
solution:

question: God commanded Hosea to take for himself a harlot as a wife to?
solution:
In the Bible, God commanded the prophet Hosea to marry Gomer, a woman who had committed sexual sins, to symbolize the covenant relationship between God and the Israelites: 

  • Symbolism

    The marriage was a prophetic sign-act that illustrated Israel's infidelity to Yahweh. The Torah views Yahweh as Israel's husband, and covenant infidelity is likened to marital unfaithfulness.

options.d: Test the faith of prophet Hosea

  • Work hard

    Paul encouraged the Thessalonians to work hard and to be proud of the work they do. 

  • Set an example

    Paul urged the Thessalonians to set an example for others by working day and night and not depending on others for support.

question: Which country did Joseph and Mary escape to with baby Jesus?
solution:
Joseph, Mary, and baby Jesus fled to Egypt to escape King Herod the Great's massacre of baby boys in Bethlehem. The story of their flight into Egypt is told in the Gospel of Matthew (Matthew 2:13–23). 

According to the Bible, an angel appeared to Joseph in a dream and told him to flee to Egypt with his family. Joseph took Mary and Jesus during the night and stayed in Egypt until Herod's death.

question: "The son of man has nowhere to lay his head" This statement was directed to?
solution:
The statement, "The Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head" was directed to a scribe who promised to follow Jesus everywhere. Jesus said this to explain that if the scribe wanted to follow him, he would have to give up his home. 

The statement appears in the Bible in Matthew 8:20 and Luke 9:58. It emphasizes that true discipleship requires a willingness to give up comfort and stability to be close to Jesus and his mission.

question: Whose sons treated the offering of the Lord with contempt
solution:
According to the biblical account in 1 Samuel 2, Eli was the High Priest of Israel, but his two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, were described as "wicked men" who had no regard for the Lord.

When people came to offer sacrifices, Eli’s sons would take the best meat for themselves by force before the fat was even burned as an offering to God. Their behaviour caused people to abhor making offerings to the Lord because the process had become corrupt.  In addition to mishandling the offerings, they were also known for acting immorally at the entrance to the tent of meeting.

Because Eli failed to restrain his sons, a prophecy was given that his family line would be cut off from the priesthood, and both Hophni and Phinehas died on the same day during a battle with the Philistines.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Daniel was cast into the den of lions because he
solution:
According to the biblical account in Daniel 6, Daniel was cast into the den of lions because he continued to pray to God despite a royal decree forbidding it.

It is important to note, however, that Daniel did not hide his actions. Jealous officials tricked King Darius into signing an irrevocable law that, for 30 days, anyone who prayed to any god or man other than the king would be thrown to the lions.

When Daniel learned of the law, he went home to his upstairs room and prayed with his windows open toward Jerusalem, just as he had always done. Rather than hiding (which would have been cowardice) or making a public spectacle (which would have been pride), he simply maintained his usual spiritual discipline in a visible way. His enemies, who were watching him, caught him in the act of praying and reported him to the king to force the execution of the law.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Moses took the bones of Joseph during the exodus because
solution:
Before Joseph died in Egypt, he made the "sons of Israel" swear that they would carry his bones out of Egypt when God eventually came to lead them to the Promised Land. This request was seen as an act of faith; Joseph believed God's promise that his descendants would one day return to the land of Canaan.

Even though hundreds of years had passed, Moses remembered this responsibility and ensured the oath was honoured during the rapid departure from Egypt.

options.b: it was their custom to carry all bones
options.c: Joseph had sworn that they should carry the bones
options.d: the bones were so precious to the Israelites

He also chose a "devout" soldier from among his personal attendants. This soldier was noted for his own piety and religious devotion.

Cornelius explained everything that had happened in his vision to these three men before sending them on to Joppa.

solution: In 1 Peter 2:16, Peter instructs Christians to live as free people, but not to use their freedom as a "cover-up for evil". Instead, they are to use that freedom to serve God by doing what is right.
