question: According to Paul in Galatians, Christians have become children of God through
solution: In Galatians 3:26, Paul writes, "So in Christ Jesus you are all children of God through faith". He argues that it is faith, not adherence to the law, that brings people into God's family.

options.a: subjecting themselves to the rule of law

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question:
I will faithfully give them their recompense, and I will make an everlasting covenant with them.

The above statement of the LORD to Israel in Isiah's prophecy suggests
solution: The promise in Isaiah 61:8 of a "faithful recompense" and an "everlasting covenant" is a message of hope and restoration for a people who had suffered.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Baal worship was introduced in Israel by
solution:
According to the accounts in 1 Kings, Jezebel was a Phoenician princess, the daughter of Ethbaal, King of Tyre. When she married King Ahab of Israel, she brought her own prophets of Baal and Asherah into the royal court.

She influenced Ahab to build a temple and an altar for Baal in the capital city of Samaria. She famously attempted to eliminate the prophets of God, leading to the major confrontation between the prophet Elijah and the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The restrictions of Ezekiel's mission to the house of Israel were to show God's
solution:
When Ezekiel was called to be a "watchman" for the house of Israel (Ezekiel 3:17), God imposed several symbolic restrictions and specific instructions on him. These restrictions, such as being struck mute for a time and being bound by cords, highlighted God's unwavering resolve and persistent nature. Despite Israel's rebellion and "hard-heartedness," God remained constant in His message and His presence among the exiles.

 Ezekiel’s silence and physical constraints were signs to the house of Israel. They demonstrated that even when communication seemed "restricted," God's plan was still actively unfolding.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The parable of the drag net, according to Jesus, was to teach His listeners about the
solution:
The parable of the dragnet (Matthew 13:47-50) is one of the "Kingdom Parables" Jesus used to explain spiritual realities. Jesus explicitly explained that the sorting of "good" and "bad" fish represents the judgment at the end of the age. Just as fishermen sort fish on the shore, Jesus stated that "the angels will come and separate the wicked from the righteous".

The parable concludes with the "bad" being thrown into a "furnace of fire". This is a symbol of the final day of reckoning for all humanity.

options.d: judgement at the close of age

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Abraham was referred to as a friend of God because he
solution:
When God commanded Abraham to leave his home or later to sacrifice his son, Isaac, Abraham did not argue or look for excuses. He trusted God completely and obeyed "on that very day".

The Scripture (James 2:23) notes that "Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness," and because of this deep relationship of trust and obedience, he was called the Friend of God.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The statement "when you give alms, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing" warns against
solution:
In Matthew 6:1-4, Jesus warns against performing righteous deeds to be seen by others. Jesus specifically condemns "hypocrites" who blow trumpets in the synagogues and streets to attract attention to their charity.

 The phrase "do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing" is a command for secrecy and humility. Jesus teaches that those who seek human praise have already received their full reward, whereas those who give in secret will be rewarded by God.

options.a: letting friends know we are giving alms
options.b: giving alms in the public space
options.c: blowing trumpets when we give alms
options.d: letting people see us when giving alms

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Jonah, in his plots to escape from the presence of God, boarded a ship at Joppa to
solution:
In the Book of Jonah, when God commanded the prophet to go to Nineveh, Jonah attempted to flee in the opposite direction. He went down to the port city of Joppa and paid the fare for a ship bound for Tarshish to "flee from the presence of the Lord".

Tarshish was likely in modern-day Spain, representing the furthest known point in the opposite direction from Nineveh (modern-day Iraq).
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Jesus changed water to wine at Cana to Galilee to
solution: According to the Gospel of John, the turning of water into wine at the wedding feast in Cana was the first of Jesus' "signs". The primary purpose, as stated in John 2:11, was to reveal His glory. As a direct result of this miraculous sign, the text notes that "His disciples believed in Him".

options.b: meet the needs of the people
options.c: manifest his glory and make the disciples believe in him

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The Mission of the Seventy signifies mission to
solution: In the Gospel of Luke, Jesus sends out seventy (or seventy-two) disciples to prepare the way for Him.  In Jewish tradition, 70 was the number of the nations of the world (from Genesis 10). While the "Twelve" often symbolised a mission to the twelve tribes of Israel, the "Seventy" signifies the universal reach of the Gospel to the Gentiles and all nations.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In his letter to the Colossians, Paul taught that when parents provoke their children, they ( children) become
solution: In Colossians 3:21, the Apostle Paul provides a specific warning to fathers (parents). "Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged". Paul taught that overbearing or harsh parenting could crush a child's spirit. This emphasises the need for encouragement within the family unit.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: On which day of the week did the appearance of Jesus take place
solution: Jesus rose from the dead on the first day of the week (Sunday). His initial appearances to Mary Magdalene and the disciples in the upper room occurred on that same first day.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: When Jesus told the would-be disciple who wanted to bury his father to leave the dead to bury their own dead, he meant that
solution: When a potential follower asked to first bury his father, Jesus replied, "Leave the dead to bury their own dead" (Matthew 8:22). This difficult saying was meant to emphasise the absolute priority of the Kingdom of God. It teaches that true discipleship entails detachment from even the most significant family obligations if they hinder one's immediate response to God's call.

options.a: this was an assurance of resurrection for all
options.b: discipleship entails detachment from the family
options.c: the man's father had not died
options.d: the spiritually dead should be left to bury the physically dead

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The Spiritual Gifts identified in first Corinthians include
solution: In 1 Corinthians 12, Paul identifies specific spiritual gifts, including wisdom, faith, healing, and prophecy.

options.a: love, joy, peace and long suffering

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question:
Have you killed and also taken possession?

The above statement by Prophet Elijah was addressed to
solution:
This statement comes from 1 Kings 21. It refers to the incident involving Naboth’s Vineyard.  King Ahab wanted a vineyard belonging to a man named Naboth, but Naboth refused to sell his ancestral inheritance.

Ahab's wife, Queen Jezebel, orchestrated a plot to have Naboth falsely accused of blasphemy and stoned to death so the King could take the land.

After Ahab went to take possession of the vineyard, God sent the Prophet Elijah to confront him. Elijah met him and delivered God's judgment, starting with the rhetorical question: "Have you killed and also taken possession?" (1 Kings 21:19).
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question:
Jesus spoke to them, saying: "I am the light of the world, whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life".

From the above statement, Jesus was trying to explain the importance in Christians'
solution:
The quote, "I am the light of the world, whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life," is found in John 8:12.

Jesus uses the metaphor of "light" to represent divine truth and guidance. "Light" represents the spiritual truth and the presence of God, while "darkness" represents sin, ignorance, and separation from God.

By following this "light," a person gains the "light of life," which refers to the spiritual understanding (knowledge of God) that guides a believer away from the death of sin and toward eternal life (salvation).

options.c: the knowledge of God, leading to salvation

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Where did Joshua deliver his farewell address to the Israelites?
solution:
This event is recorded in Joshua 24. Near the end of his life, Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel at Shechem for a final covenant renewal. Shechem was a deeply symbolic place for the Israelites, as it was where God first promised the land to Abraham.

Joshua reviewed God's faithfulness to the nation and famously challenged the people to choose whom they would serve, stating, "But as for me and my household, we will serve the Lord" (Joshua 24:15).
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question:
...he who touches the earth and it melts...?

Who was Prophet Amos referring to in the statement above?
solution:
The statement "...he who touches the earth and it melts..." is a direct quote from Amos 9:5. In this passage, the Prophet Amos uses powerful imagery to describe the supreme authority and awesome power of God:

The verse explicitly names the subject as "The Lord GOD of hosts" (or "The Lord, the LORD Almighty" in some translations). The term "hosts" refers to God as the commander of heavenly armies.

The "melting" of the earth symbolises that creation itself cannot stand before God's presence or judgment. Even the firmest ground is described as liquefying or quaking at His mere touch.

Amos uses this description to remind the Israelites that no one can hide or escape from God's judgment, as He has complete control over the entire universe.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: David spared the life of Saul but took away his
solution:
This took place in the Wilderness of Ziph. While King Saul and his army were sleeping, David and Abishai crept into Saul’s camp. Abishai wanted to kill Saul with his own spear, but David refused to harm "the Lord’s anointed."

Instead of killing him, David took Saul’s spear and the jar of water that were near his head as proof that he had the chance to take the King's life but chose mercy instead.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question:
"No church entered into partnership with me in giving and receiving except only you"

The above statement of Paul was meant for the
solution:
In Philippians 4:15, Paul wrote this letter to the church at Philippi to thank them for their consistent financial and material support during his missionary journeys and his imprisonment. Paul specifies that this unique partnership began "in the early days of your acquaintance with the gospel, when I set out from Macedonia."

 While Paul supported himself through tentmaking to avoid being a burden, he accepted the gifts from the Philippians because they were sent out of love and a shared commitment to the ministry.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Paul adjoins believers in Corinthians to forgive one another so that
solution: This instruction is found in 2 Corinthians 2:10-11. Paul encourages the church to forgive and comfort an individual who had caused grief to the community. He explains that forgiveness is necessary "in order that Satan might not outwit us; for we are not unaware of his schemes." Paul's concern was that bitterness or lack of mercy would create a foothold for spiritual division within the church.

options.a: Satan will not gain advantage over us

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: According to the prophecy of Ezekiel, all souls belong to
solution: This is stated in Ezekiel 18:4: "Behold, all souls are mine; the soul of the father as well as the soul of the son is mine...". In this chapter, God uses the prophet Ezekiel to explain individual accountability, making it clear that every human life is His creation and under His authority.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: The women of Samaria were described by Amos as
solution: In Amos 4:1, the prophet uses this metaphor to rebuke the wealthy women of Samaria. Bashan was a region known for its lush pastures and well-fed livestock; Amos used the term to criticise the women for their luxury, greed and oppression of the poor.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: Josiah's reward for being penitent and remorseful when Judah sinned against the LORD by worshipping other gods was
solution: In 2 Kings 22:18–20 and 2 Chronicles 34:26–28, the prophetess Huldah delivers a message from God to King Josiah. Because his heart was "tender and penitent," God promised that the disaster coming upon Jerusalem would not happen during his lifetime; instead, he would be "gathered to [his] grave in peace" and his eyes would not see the evil brought upon the place.

options.a: he was not going to witness the desolation
options.b: he was going to rebuild the Temple
options.c: Judah shall no longer be destroyed
options.d: his family will be saved from the forthcoming pestilence

subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question:
"You pity the plant, for which you did not labour, nor did you make it grow which came into being in a night, and perished in a night".

The statment above was directed to
solution: This is found in Jonah 4:10. After Jonah became angry that God spared the city of Nineveh, God used a fast-growing plant that withered overnight to teach him a lesson about compassion. God's point was that if Jonah could feel pity for a plant he didn't even grow, God should certainly have compassion for a great city filled with people.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In Jeremiah, the sovereignty of God is symbolised in the
solution: In Jeremiah 18:1–6, God instructs the prophet to go to a potter's house. Seeing the potter reshape a marred vessel on the wheel served as a visual parable. It demonstrated that just as a potter has absolute authority over the clay, God has the sovereign right to shape, break, or rebuild nations and individuals according to His will.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)

question: In the book of Prophet Jeremiah, God promised to write the New Covenant on
solution: This prophecy is found in Jeremiah 31:33. Unlike the old covenant written on stone tablets, God promised a new era where He would put His law within His people and write it on their hearts, leading to a direct, personal relationship with Him.
subject: Christian Religious Knowledge (CRK)
